summary: I was born in California in 1944, specifically in Turlock, which is in the Central Valley. Growing up in California meant being around a lot of farm life and hard work, especially in my early years. Later, my family moved to the San Francisco Bay Area, where I spent my high school years and started my career.

I was born in Turlock, California, in 1944. It’s a small town in the Central Valley where I grew up surrounded by farm life. As a kid, I did a lot of hunting, fishing, and helping my dad with tractor work and hauling hay, which was tough but rewarding.

The Central Valley is where I spent my early childhood, growing up in Turlock. It was a farming region, and my family was involved in contract work and hay baling. Life there was all about hard work on the land, but it was a great place to grow up with plenty of outdoor activities.

My dad’s parents came from Australia, so that’s part of my family background. It’s interesting because most people assume European roots, but my paternal side is Australian. This heritage was always part of my family story growing up.

On my mom’s side, we’re basically Irish, though I’m not exactly sure when or how they came over. My family has been in the United States for a long time, probably since the early days of settlement. So, Irish roots are definitely part of my heritage.

Along with Irish roots, my family also has English ancestry, probably on my mom’s side. Like with the Irish, the family has been here in the U.S. for many generations, so the English connection is part of my background. It’s just part of the mix of where my family came from.

When I was about 14 or 15, my family moved to the San Francisco Bay Area, specifically south of San Francisco in Redwood City. This move was because my dad switched from seasonal farm work to construction, which was more stable. I went to high school there before quitting at 16 to start working.

Redwood City is where I spent my high school years after moving from Turlock. I went to school there for a couple of years before quitting at 16 to become a carpenter apprentice. Later, I worked at a Montgomery Ward warehouse located in Redwood City before joining the waterfront.

I joined the United States Navy at 17 in 1961, following my brother’s footsteps and wanting to see the world. I served as a diesel mechanic on a fleet tug, doing engine room and refrigeration maintenance. I stayed on the same ship for my entire service until I got out in 1966, after an extension due to the Kennedy assassination.

Although I did four tours to Vietnam, technically the war hadn’t officially started when I was there, according to records. My ship took supplies up the rivers and we got shot at a few times by snipers, but we never saw direct combat. We spent time in places like Saigon and had R&R stops in Hong Kong and Hawaii during the conflict.

I served on a fleet tug in the Navy, which was an ocean-going tugboat that carried supplies and equipment. I was responsible for diesel engine maintenance and refrigeration repairs on board. The crew changed several times, but I stayed on the same vessel from boot camp until I left the service.

While on my tours in Vietnam, we docked in Saigon where I witnessed some street fights and general unrest. One time, a fight spilled into the water and I watched with a few others. It was part of the experience of being in Vietnam, though I didn’t see much direct combat action there.

During my Navy service, we sometimes followed Russian trawlers and used radio jamming equipment to monitor them. It was part of our duties while in the Pacific and Southeast Asia region. Sometimes the crew would go swimming when these encounters ended, so it was a mix of tension and downtime.

Hong Kong was one of the places we stopped for rest and relaxation during my Navy tours in the Vietnam era. It was a welcome break from the river runs and supply missions. We got to enjoy some time off there before heading back to duty.

Hawaii was another stop on the way to and from Vietnam during my Navy service. We would take breaks there to rest before continuing our missions. It was a nice change of pace from the intense river work in Southeast Asia.

The Philippines was one of the countries we visited during my Navy service in the Pacific. It was part of the circuit of ports including Japan, Hong Kong, and Guam where we stopped during our tours. These stops gave us a chance to see a bit of the world beyond the ship.

Japan was a regular port of call during my Navy service, and I even took a train trip up to Tokyo while I was there. It was one of the more memorable experiences of seeing the world while in the Navy. We also stopped in other places like the Philippines and Guam on these tours.

While stationed in Japan during my Navy service, I took the opportunity to travel by train up to Tokyo. It was a chance to experience a big city and see something different from the usual ship routine. That trip stands out as a highlight of my time overseas.

The assassination of John F. Kennedy in 1963 had a direct impact on my Navy service because my discharge was extended by four months due to the event. I was supposed to get out in September 1963, but the extension kept me on the ship during a Vietnam deployment. It was a strange time, and that extension meant I got to see Vietnam firsthand before leaving the Navy.

After leaving the Navy in 1966, I worked in the warehouse at Montgomery Ward in Redwood City. It was a non-union job and pretty low-key, just me and an older lady doing pricing and warehouse work. It was a step before I moved on to the waterfront and the ILWU.

I joined the ILWU in 1967 when I started working on the waterfront. It was my first union job, and I quickly learned about the unique culture there. The union was a different world with its own politics and social justice causes, and I became comfortable working alongside the longshoremen despite some old-timers being tough on newcomers.

I didn’t meet Jim Brown right away after joining the waterfront. We actually connected in the early 1970s through a bar I owned that was across the street from the union hall. Jim Brown eventually bought the bar from another longshoreman, Jim Fowler, and that’s how our friendship started.

Jim Fowler was a longshoreman who owned the bar I had before Jim Brown bought it. I sold the bar to Jim Fowler, and later he sold it to Jim Brown. Through this chain, I eventually met Jim Brown and developed a friendship with him in the early 1970s.
